contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: For one query, a scan or direct one-pass hash may dominate preprocessing; for repeated workloads, compare the full build-plus-query cost and update invalidation of hashing and sorting.

items.prompt: An unsorted array receives exactly one exact-membership query, and no later reuse is possible. Which baseline should be considered first?

options.text: A direct O(n) scan with O(1) auxiliary space.
Always sort in O(n log n) before one query.
Always build an O(n)-space hash set because expected lookup is O(1).
Binary-search the unsorted array.

feedback.reason: For one query, preprocessing must earn back its cost inside the same workload.
feedback.details: A scan is often simplest and asymptotically no worse than building a hash set once.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_first: Preprocessing may cost more than the single scan.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_always: The build still scans all n values and uses extra memory.

items.prompt: A static array will receive millions of arbitrary-order exact-membership queries. Which signal strongly favors hashing?

options.text: One expected O(n) set build can serve expected O(1) queries.
Ordered grouping is the primary result.
Repeated O(n) scans are preferable regardless of q.
Input mutation is the only relevant factor.

feedback.reason: A large repeated equality workload can amortize hash preprocessing.
feedback.details: Memory, collision guarantees, and updates still belong in the final comparison.

items.prompt: A static dataset receives many ordered range queries whose answers must be emitted in value order. Which preprocessing is naturally reusable?

options.text: Sort once, then locate boundaries and traverse each requested range.
Use only a plain hash set.
Scan all values for every range without considering q.
Store only counts and discard values.

feedback.reason: The repeated query relation is ordered, so sorted preprocessing is directly reusable.
feedback.details: Hashing may assist equality, but it does not replace the order structure required by range queries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_only: A set does not provide ordered range boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_each: Repeated full scans can be much more expensive.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_only: Range output needs the values or ordered keys.

items.prompt: The dataset changes after almost every query. What must be included before choosing preprocessing?

options.text: The cost and legality of maintaining or rebuilding the hash or sorted representation after updates.
Only the query cost after a hypothetical free build.
A sorted snapshot remains correct after arbitrary updates.
A hash table cannot support updates.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing is useful only while the derived representation remains valid.
feedback.details: Dynamic workloads require build, update, query, and memory costs in one model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_once_forever: Updates can invalidate order and contents.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_static: It can, but update costs and guarantees matter.

items.prompt: The future number of queries is unknown and may be zero. Which design can be reasonable?

options.text: Start with direct scans and build a reusable representation only after observed workload justifies it, if the contract permits.
Always pay the largest preprocessing cost immediately.
An unknown q makes analysis impossible.
Choose hashing because queries are mentioned.

feedback.reason: An adaptive strategy can avoid sunk preprocessing when demand is absent.
feedback.details: The transition point should be based on measured or modeled cumulative cost.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.eager_always: The work may never be reused.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_strategy: Threshold or adaptive strategies can be modeled.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_word: The actual volume and relation remain unknown.

items.prompt: A single pass must detect any duplicate, and memory is plentiful. Does this alone prove hashing is the only valid strategy?

options.text: No. Hashing is expected linear, but sorting and scanning can also be correct if mutation and O(n log n) time are acceptable.
Yes. Duplicate detection cannot use sorting.
Yes, because the phrase single pass forbids all preprocessing choices.
Yes, comparing adjacent unsorted values is always enough.

feedback.reason: A one-off duplicate query may have several correct strategies.
feedback.details: The decision depends on time budget, memory, mutation, online stopping, and guarantee requirements.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_one_pass_word: The implementation constraint must be explicit; the problem result alone does not prove one family.

items.prompt: The same key-count query is repeated many times over immutable data. Which preprocessing avoids recomputing counts?

options.text: Build key counts once and answer each equality-count query from the map.
Resort the data for every query.
Recount the entire array for every key without considering q.
Delete the count table after every answer.

feedback.reason: The repeated query asks for equality-keyed aggregate state.
feedback.details: One frequency map build amortizes across all later count lookups.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_each_query: The same preprocessing should be reused.

items.prompt: Input is already sorted and receives many exact-membership queries. What should the analysis credit?

options.text: The sorted representation already exists, so binary search needs no new sorting cost.
Charge O(n log n) sorting before every analysis.
Build a hash set because expected O(1) always dominates existing order.
Ignore binary search because hashing is the only reusable method.

feedback.reason: Cost comparison begins from the actual representation supplied by the contract.
feedback.details: Existing sorted order can make binary search attractive without any preprocessing or mutation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_cost_again: Known input state is reusable preprocessing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_required: The memory and build may not be justified.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_only: Sorted order directly supports logarithmic queries.

items.prompt: Which workload facts should be modeled before preprocessing? Select all that apply.

options.text: Number and type of queries.
Frequency and cost of updates or invalidation.
Whether data is already sorted or indexed.
Whether the query function is named lookup.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing decisions depend on the complete lifecycle of the derived representation.
feedback.details: Query vocabulary alone is insufficient.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_name: Naming does not determine workload cost.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.q: Reuse depends on volume and relation.

items.prompt: Which situations often favor a direct one-pass or one-query approach? Select all that apply.

options.text: Exactly one query with no future reuse.
Very small input where setup and allocation dominate.
An online query can terminate before the stream ends.
Millions of repeated equality queries on static data.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing is weakest when reuse is absent or early streaming termination is valuable.
feedback.details: Large static repeated workloads are the opposite case.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.one_query: Build cost may not be recovered.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.stream_early_exit: Offline sorting cannot exploit the same early stop.

items.prompt: Which repeated workloads directly reuse a hash table's equality-keyed state? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many exact membership queries.
Many key-frequency queries.
Frequent insert/delete updates on hashable keys when expected table updates are acceptable.
Many ordered range reports in key order.

feedback.reason: Hash preprocessing is reusable for equality-keyed queries and updates.
feedback.details: Ordered range workloads consume sorted order instead.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_ranges: A plain hash table lacks range structure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.membership: Expected O(1) lookup directly serves them.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.updates_hashable: A hash table can maintain equality state incrementally.

items.prompt: Which repeated workloads directly consume a sorted representation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many exact queries when the data is already sorted or sorting is acceptable.
Many lower-bound, upper-bound, or range-report queries.
Repeated deterministic ordered traversals.
One online first-repeat query before the stream ends.

feedback.reason: Sorted preprocessing is strongest when the query relation is ordered or the representation already exists.
feedback.details: Online arrival-order queries favor incremental hash state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_first_seen: Full sorted preprocessing conflicts with the online contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_output: The sorted representation is already in result order.

items.prompt: Arrange the workload-modeling process.

elements.text: List builds, queries, updates, and required outputs.
Introduce n, q, update count, distinct-key count, and total reported output where relevant.
Derive complete hash, sort, and direct-scan costs under aligned assumptions.
Choose a strategy or adaptive threshold that satisfies correctness and minimizes the relevant workload cost.

feedback.reason: Query-volume decisions require cumulative rather than per-operation analysis.
feedback.details: The break-even point follows from complete cost expressions.

items.prompt: Arrange a reusable hash-membership workflow.

elements.text: Scan n values and build the set.
Keep the set valid while the dataset remains unchanged or maintain it through legal updates.
Answer each exact membership query from the set.
Release the representation after the workload ends.

feedback.reason: The one-time build is amortized only while the retained state remains valid and reused.
feedback.details: Memory lifetime is part of the preprocessing tradeoff.

items.prompt: Arrange a reusable sorted-query workflow.

elements.text: Reuse existing sorted order or sort a legal representation once.
Keep the sorted representation valid across the query workload.
Use binary-search boundaries for each query.
Traverse and emit any required ordered range, then account for output size.

feedback.reason: Sorting preprocessing creates reusable order for both exact and range queries.
feedback.details: Range output cost remains in addition to logarithmic boundary location.

items.prompt: Answer one exact-membership query by scanning an unsorted array of n values. Select worst-case time and auxiliary space.
constraints: No preprocessing or retained index is available.

feedback.reason: A missing target can require inspecting every value.
feedback.details: The scan needs only an index or iterator and comparison state.

items.prompt: Answer q independent exact-membership queries by rescanning the same unsorted n-value array each time. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: No preprocessing is retained between queries.

feedback.reason: Without reusable state, worst-case linear query cost repeats q times.
feedback.details: The approach minimizes memory but may be too slow for large q.

items.prompt: Build a hash set once from n values and answer q exact membership queries under expected O(1) operations. Select expected total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The set remains valid for all queries.

feedback.reason: The build cost is paid once and expected constant lookup is reused.
feedback.details: The table remains live for the query workload.

items.prompt: Sort n values once and answer q exact queries by binary search. Select total time and auxiliary space for an in-place O(n log n) sort using O(log n) stack.

time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

time.sort_and_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: The one-time ordering cost is followed by logarithmic queries.
feedback.details: The path also provides reusable ordered traversal not offered by a plain hash set.

items.prompt: Compare q full scans, O(qn), with sorting once and binary-searching, O(n log n + q log n). What asymptotic query volume is sufficient for the sorting path to break even when n is large?
constraints: Assume comparison and scan primitive costs are comparable.
Use an asymptotic threshold rather than an exact machine-dependent constant.

feedback.reason: Equating qn with n log n + q log n gives q approximately n log n divided by n - log n, which is Θ(log n).
feedback.details: Exact crossover depends on constants, existing order, mutation, and query semantics.

items.prompt: A dataset is rebuilt completely u times. Across all versions, q exact queries are answered. Select expected hash-workload time and sorted-workload time.
constraints: Each hash rebuild costs expected O(n), and each hash query expected O(1).
Each sorted rebuild costs O(n log n), and each binary-search query O(log n).

sort_time: O(un log n + q log n)
O(un log n + q log n)

sort_time.resorts_and_queries: O(un log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: Frequent complete invalidation multiplies preprocessing by u.
feedback.details: A realistic dynamic comparison must include that lifecycle cost.

items.prompt: Sort n values once, answer q ordered range queries with two binary-search boundaries each, and emit T total values across all query results. Select total time and output space.
constraints: Each boundary search costs O(log n).
T counts all materialized reported values.

time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n + T)
O(n log n + q log n + T)

time.sort_boundaries_output: O(n log n + q log n + T)

feedback.reason: Ordered preprocessing reduces boundary location but cannot remove the cost of emitting required results.
feedback.details: This is a workload where sorting provides a relation that a plain hash table does not.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: For one query, a scan or direct one-pass hash may dominate preprocessing; for repeated workloads, compare the full build-plus-query cost and update invalidation of hashing and sorting.
